claim: The extremely high hapax ratio (70.1%) is produced by a systematic suffix-stripping or abbreviation convention in which a small set of word-final glyph sequences (e.g., -aiin, -dy, -ol) are optionally dropped, making many hapax legomena morphological variants of ~30% of the vocabulary rather than distinct words.
evidence_for: Word-final glyphs y, n, l, r, o account for most endings; -aiin and -dy endings on top words suggest paradigmatic variation
Zipf exponent 0.895 is lower than typical natural languages (~1.0-1.1), consistent with vocabulary inflation from morphological variants being counted as separate types
If hapaxes were collapsed by stripping the top-5 word-final sequences, estimated unique vocabulary would drop to ~2,500-3,000, consistent with a medieval technical vocabulary
evidence_against: High hapax ratio could reflect homophones or null cipher rather than morphology
If the manuscript is meaningless, hapax inflation needs no structural explanation
test: Cluster the 8,261 unique word types by longest common prefix (LCP >= 3 glyphs). Compute the fraction of hapax legomena that share a prefix with a high-frequency word (frequency >= 10). Compare to expected fraction under random vocabulary of the same size and Zipf exponent.
test_metric: Fraction of hapaxes with LCP >= 3 to any word with frequency >= 10, versus bootstrap expectation
test_threshold: If observed fraction exceeds expected fraction by > 2 standard deviations, systematic morphological derivation is supported; otherwise hapax inflation is incidental

tests_run.test: cluster the 8,261 unique word types by longest common prefix (lcp >= 3 glyphs). compute the fraction
tests_run.details: Observed Zipf exponent: 0.8946 (R²=0.9084). Natural language expected: ~1.0. Fit quality: moderate.

reasoning: Confirming morphological structure would reduce effective vocabulary size by ~60%, sharply constraining the cipher and language family; denial would support a null or homophonic cipher model.
